title: Last chance saloon: Assessing the Giro d’Italia GC battle with one mountain stage to go
content:
Cyclist
Last chance saloon: Assessing the Giro d’Italia GC battle with one mountain stage to go

The Queen Stage of the 2026 Giro d’Italia is done and dusted and just one mountain day stands between the riders and Rome.

Before one final battle for positions takes place on Piancavallo, which spots are up for grabs and which riders will be looking over their shoulder on Saturday’s stage?

Jonas Vingegaard (Visma-Lease a Bike)

Some could say the Trofeo Senza Fine already had Jonas Vingegaard’s name half engraved on it as soon as he announced his plans to debut and, while it has been predictable, Visma-Lease a Bike have done a solid job of getting their leader where he needs to be to launch his stage-winning attacks.

Having navigated the Queen Stage with ease as teammate Sepp Kuss claimed victory, this will surely end with a first maglia rosa and Vingegaard’s name in the elite club of having won all three Grand Tours.

  • Current position: 1st overall

Felix Gall (Decathlon CMA CGM)

Felix Gall is on track for his best result in a Grand Tour. He’s really stepped up a level at this Giro, being first behind Vingegaard and leading the rest of the pack, and the descending issue that saw him plummet down the Volta a Catalunya GC hasn’t reared its head.  

He currently sits second overall, blowing his fifth place at the 2025 Tour finish out of the water. He attacked again from the maglia rosa group towards the end of Stage 19 and with one final day in the mountains left, is looking very comfortable indeed in second.

  • Current position: 2nd overall (+4min 03sec)

Jai Hindley (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe)

2022 Giro winner Jai Hindley entered the race as co-leader with Giulio Pellizzari, which turned out to be a good plan as the Italian haemorrhaged time early on. Hindley meanwhile shot into fourth overall on Stage 7’s mammoth day to Blockhaus, where he previously won a stage in 2022. He has since jumped up to the final podium spot following Stage 19 in the Dolomites having matched Vingegaard and Gall and is showing no signs of slipping while his nearest challenger struggles.

He”ll have to get the job done on Saturday though, as only half a minute separates him from Netcompany Ineos’s Thymen Arensman.

  • Current position: 3rd overall (+5min 04sec)

Thymen Arensman (Netcompany Ineos)

This is shaping up to be Thymen Arensman’s best Grand Tour finish too. Having finished sixth at the Giro in both 2023 and 2024, he’s been climbing well this year and put in a fantastic time-trial, finishing second and the best of the GC contenders to shoot up onto the podium.

There have been a few cracks appearing in the latter stages of the race though, losing his podium spot to Jai Hindley on today’s Queen Stage. It wasn’t a major catastrophe, but he has a big task ahead if he wants to get it back.

Still, fourth would be a career best, so it won’t be too big a disappointment if Hindley stays ahead,

  • Current position: 4th overall (+5min 33sec)

Derek Gee-West (Lidl-Trek)

Derek Gee-West loves the Giro. The Canadian had a breakthrough ride here in 2023 and returned to finish fourth in 2025. Now, with Lidl-Trek, he currently sits fifth overall and has found his best form at the business end of the race.

He put in a huge shift on Stage 19 to take a chunk of time on his rivals and set up a potential last ditch run at the podium tomorrow. He did used to be teammates with Chris Froome…

  • Current position: 5th overall (+6min 31sec)

Afonso Eulálio (Bahrain Victorious)

What a race this has been for Afonso Eulálio. The 24-year-old turned things around for Bahrain Victorious and then some after their team leader Santiago Buitrago crashed out on Stage 2.

He rode into the pink jersey from the breakaway on Stage 5, held off Vingegaard until Stage 13 and is still riding with dogged determination to not only finish high on GC but win the white jersey.

He has to be careful tomorrow though. Visma’s Davide Piganzoli is a minute behind him in the white jersey fight and is creeping upwards, crossing the line of today’s stage over a minute ahead of him. It’s likely to go down to the wire.

  • Current position: 6th overall (+7min 26sec)

Michael Storer (Tudor Pro Cycling)

Michael Storer is yet another rider set for a best-ever Grand Tour finish, having topped out at tenth at the Giro in both 2024 and 2025. With just one mountain stage to go he sits in seventh place on GC and is still holding strong form, finishing tenth on today’s stage ahead of Eulálio, who is now just 24 seconds ahead.

With young French teammate Mathys Rondel currently in 11th, only 22 seconds behind Egan Bernal one place in front, Tudor could well finish this race with two riders in the top ten overall.

  • Current position: 7th overall (+7min 50sec)

Davide Piganzoli (Visma-Lease a Bike)

One of the revelations of the Giro, 23-year-old Davide Piganzoli flexed his talents with a third place on Corno alle Scale, then followed this performance up with a fourth place at Pila and sixth in Carì. He has burst into life, continuing to ride to impressive finishes after pulling for Vingegaard, who has stated the team would love to get the young Italian the white jersey in Rome.

No matter where he finishes, this is a massive moment for both Piganzoli and Visma-Lease a Bike, who may have been scratching their heads after Simon Yates retired at the start of the year but now have another certified super-domestique and potential Grand Tour leader.

  • Current position: 8th overall (+8min 29sec)

For more on the Giro d’Italia, visit our hub page.

author: Robyn Davidson | Editorial Assistant
